Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose most of the time. I'd usually dedicate chunks of time on weekends and a few evenings during the week, aiming for around 30 hours total. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time making notes and rewriting concepts. The routine existed, but I wouldn't say I was passionate about it—it was more like going through the motions because I knew I had to.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. When assignments were coming up, I'd cram in more hours, but during quieter weeks I'd slack off a bit. I tried to keep some consistency, but life stuff and my general lack of enthusiasm made that hard. Deadlines were really the main thing that kept me on track.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or seeing a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I was missing anything important. I wasn't one of those students who logged in out of genuine curiosity—it was more about staying on top of requirements so I wouldn't fall behind.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became kind of automatic to check the LMS in the morning with my coffee. I think the peer support group I was part of helped with that—we'd remind each other about new content or discussions. It wasn't exciting, but it became a habit, like checking email or something.
Q5: The reading materials and written guides were most useful to me since that's how I learn best. I also found the discussion forums helpful when people actually posted substantive stuff—it gave me different perspectives on the material. The video lectures were okay, but I preferred reading through transcripts when they were available.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the optional supplementary videos and some of the extra practice quizzes. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what would actually count toward my grade. If something wasn't directly tied to an assessment, it often got pushed aside. I just didn't have the energy for extras.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I'd be assessed on, or if it genuinely helped clarify a confusing concept. I also valued things that were efficient—if I could learn the same thing in less time, that was better. Anything that felt like busywork just drained me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't really afford. Required stuff had to get done no matter what, so there was a different kind of pressure there. Optional things felt nice in theory, but when I was already feeling low on energy and motivation, they were the first to go. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but I rarely did.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of PDFs and readings early on and studied from those on my tablet without being connected. I also used my own handwritten notes extensively—I'd rewrite key concepts multiple times to memorize them. Some weeks, most of my actual learning happened completely offline.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd spend hours reviewing my printed notes or working through problems on paper, and none of that would show up anywhere in the system. I'd also discuss concepts with my peer support group in person or over text, which was really valuable but invisible to the LMS.
Q11: Yeah, that was pretty common for me. I'd download lecture slides or readings once, then work through them multiple times over several days without logging back in. It was more convenient that way, especially when my internet was spotty or I was studying somewhere without good wifi.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up. I'd feel this sadness creeping in, like what's the point of all this. My resilience isn't great, so when things got tough, I'd withdraw a bit. Having the peer support helped me push through, but some weeks I was just going through the motions.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one for me. With limited resources, I was juggling a lot outside of school, and sometimes I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Confusion with certain topics also made me avoid those sections until I absolutely had to face them.
Q14: Oh, for sure. There were times I'd watch videos or scroll through readings but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd finish a video and realize I couldn't remember a single thing from it. That happened more often when I was feeling down or overwhelmed.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they felt performative and exhausting. I knew they mattered for participation, but putting my thoughts out there when I was feeling sad or unmotivated was hard. I'd also delay starting big assignments because the thought of them felt overwhelming, even though I knew procrastinating made it worse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator. Without them, I probably would have drifted even more. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly kick into gear, cramming everything in at the last minute. It wasn't healthy, but it worked—my exam scores and grades turned out fine despite my scattered approach.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, though not always in good ways. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to avoid that type of work. I'd take a while to process negative comments before I could use them constructively. My response time to feedback was pretty slow because of that.
Q18: The course structure was actually pretty clear, which helped someone like me who needs external scaffolding. Having set modules and deadlines gave me a framework to follow even when I wasn't feeling it. Without that structure, I would have been completely lost.
Q19: The reading-heavy format worked well for my learning style, which made engagement easier. But some of the interactive elements felt forced, like mandatory discussion posts that didn't really add much. Having peer support built into the course was helpful though—it gave me accountability partners.
Q20: I think sometimes people are searching for something, trying to understand material but struggling to produce output. Or maybe they're dealing with stuff outside of school that makes it hard to complete work even when they're trying. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just anxious clicking around.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My LMS activity was decent but not excessive, yet I scored really well on exams. A lot of my real learning happened offline with my notes and downloaded materials. The system can't see you sitting quietly with a notebook, actually understanding things.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just going through motions. They definitely miss all the offline work and the emotional state behind your actions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious and want to dig deeper, when learning feels meaningful rather than like a chore. For me, real engagement was rare this semester—I performed well, but I often felt disconnected from the whole process.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how sad I felt most of the time, or how much the peer support group kept me afloat. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes, or the conversations with classmates that helped concepts click. It also wouldn't show that despite my good grades, I often felt like I was just drifting through without real purpose.
